items.q: What is the capacity of short-term memory according to Miller?
items.why: Students confuse capacity (items) with duration (about 18-30 seconds without rehearsal). Miller's magic number describes how many CHUNKS, not how long.

items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.
Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: Miller (1956) estimated short-term memory capacity at:
items.why: It's 7 plus or minus 2. “3 plus or minus 1” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The case of HM provided support for the multi-store model because he:
items.why: It's Lost LTM but kept STM. “Lost STM but kept LTM” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many seconds is STM duration in Peterson and Peterson?
items.why: The real figure is 18 seconds. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
